hook_banner.title: Mnemos hooks not installed
hook_banner.body: Without these, sessions won't auto-ingest and Claude won't get project context on next launch. Install them with one command:
hook_banner.no_settings: (No ~/.claude/settings.json found yet — running install will create one.)
hook_banner.install_success: Mnemos hooks installed. Restart Claude Code sessions to take effect.

hook_errors.title: {{count}} recent hook error(s)

detectors.hook_silence: {{count}} recent Claude Code session(s) detected, but no ingest job has succeeded in the last 6h. Check that hooks are installed.

pages_browser.truncation_hint: Showing first 1000 pages. Use search to narrow results.

empty.title: No sessions ingested yet

bulk_import.banner_text: You have {{n}} lost sessions for this project.

activity.title: Activity
activity.undo_modal_desc: This will revert all pages affected by this operation to their state before it ran.

empty.title: No operations yet
empty.body: Every change to the vault — ingest, page edit, merge, delete, lint autofix — gets logged here with one-click undo. Empty means the vault hasn't been touched yet.

trash.title: Trash

empty.title: Nothing deleted
empty.body: Pages you remove (manually or via ontology / lint autofix) land here for 30 days before permanent cleanup. You can restore from here at any time within that window.

trash.restore_modal_desc: Move {{name}} back to its original location.
trash.delete_permanent_modal_desc: This cannot be undone. Type the page name to confirm.

bulk.cleanup_blocked_confirm_body: This permanently deletes {{n}} entries with missing or invalid metadata. Cannot be undone.

empty.title: No snapshots yet

suggestions.title: Suggestions

empty.title: No proposals pending

intro.heading: What claude-mnemos is and why you need it
intro.body:
claude-mnemos is your long-term memory for Claude Code. While Claude Code only holds context within a single session, mnemos stores every conversation as structured markdown wiki pages in your vault. Every new session start — and the relevant pages are auto-injected into Claude's context.

In short: you keep coding with Claude Code as usual. Mnemos sits alongside, reads finished sessions, turns them into atomic wiki pages (entities, concepts, sources), and when you launch Claude again — injects the most relevant ones at startup.

How it works technically — two operations:
• **Ingest** (after a session) — you closed Claude Code → mnemos reads the session file → runs it through an LLM → extracts atomic facts into markdown pages.
• **Inject** (before a session) — you launch Claude in the same folder → a hook sees the start → searches your vault for relevant pages → automatically adds them to the system prompt. Claude effectively "remembers" what you did before.

What you get:
• Claude "remembers" what you did in this project a month ago, doesn't ask the same things twice.
• Vault is plain markdown files. Open in Obsidian, grep, commit to git.
• Works through your Pro/Max subscription — no separate API key, no extra billing.
• Everything is local. The vault sits on your disk, doesn't go anywhere outside (except the LLM calls during ingest).

What mnemos does NOT do (common misconceptions):
• Does NOT watch you in real-time. Only looks at closed sessions post-factum.
• Does NOT write into the active Claude session (no impersonation, no command execution).
• Does NOT send your vault anywhere. Only the ingest process talks to the Claude API/subscription.
• Does NOT replace git. Commit your vault separately for versioning and backup.
• Does NOT need internet for UI/browsing — only for ingesting new sessions.

If you're just starting — head to Quickstart. If everything's installed — see Workflows.

quickstart.heading: Quickstart
quickstart.intro: Six steps from zero to a working mnemos. Stuck? Check "If things break" below.
quickstart.step1_title: 1. Verify Claude Code is logged in
quickstart.step1_body:
In a terminal: claude --version — should show the version. If not — install Claude Code from https://claude.ai/download.

Next: claude login — browser auth. Mnemos uses your Pro/Max (or Max $200) subscription for ingest, no separate API key needed.

Verify login works: claude -p "hi" — Claude should reply "hi" or similar within a few seconds.
quickstart.step2_body:
Mnemos isn't on PyPI yet — install from source. The recommended path uses pipx (an industry-standard isolated installer for Python CLI tools). Plain pip-in-venv is documented as a fallback for users who already manage their own venvs.

A. Get the source.

Windows (PowerShell):
  git clone https://github.com/<your-github-username>/claude-mnemos.git D:\code\claude-mnemos
  cd D:\code\claude-mnemos

macOS / Linux:
  git clone https://github.com/<your-github-username>/claude-mnemos.git ~/code/claude-mnemos
  cd ~/code/claude-mnemos

B. Install via pipx (recommended).

If you don't have pipx yet:
  Windows:        py -3.12 -m pip install --user pipx
  macOS / Linux:  python3 -m pip install --user pipx

Then add pipx to your PATH (reopen terminal afterwards):
  Windows:        py -3.12 -m pipx ensurepath
  macOS / Linux:  python3 -m pipx ensurepath

Now install mnemos editable:
  pipx install --editable . --python 3.12

This puts mnemos in an isolated venv:
  Windows:        %USERPROFILE%\pipx\venvs\claude-mnemos\
  macOS / Linux:  ~/.local/share/pipx/venvs/claude-mnemos/
And publishes the global commands `mnemos`, `mnemos-mcp`, `mnemos-tray` on your PATH (~/.local/bin).

Why pipx (and not pip install -e . into system Python):
• Isolated venv — installing other packages later can never break mnemos's dependencies.
• Editable mode preserved — `git pull` picks up code changes immediately, no reinstall.
• Clean uninstall: `pipx uninstall claude-mnemos` removes everything, no orphan files.
• To upgrade later: `cd <path> && git pull && pipx reinstall claude-mnemos`.

C. Fallback: pip in your own venv.

If you'd rather manage a venv yourself:
  python3.12 -m venv .venv
  source .venv/bin/activate         (macOS / Linux)
  .venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1        (Windows PowerShell)
  pip install -e .

You'll then need to activate that venv every time before running mnemos commands. pipx avoids that step entirely.

Avoid: `pip install -e .` directly into your system Python. It works at first, but every future `pip install <other-package>` may conflict with mnemos's pinned deps; partial uninstalls leave orphan files behind; PATH conflicts compound. pipx exists exactly to prevent this.

D. Verify.
  mnemos --version
  mnemos --help

You should see subcommands (project, daemon, tray, settings, ingest, …).

Python requirement: 3.12+. Check: `python --version` (or `python3 --version` on mac/linux). Pass `--python 3.12` to pipx explicitly so it doesn't pick up an older interpreter on systems with multiple Pythons.
quickstart.step3_title: 3. Start the daemon and open the dashboard
quickstart.step3_body:
In a terminal: mnemos daemon start — spawns a background process on 127.0.0.1:5757. It serves the dashboard, watches vaults, runs the job queue.

Open http://localhost:5757/ in a browser — you'll see the Overview with an (initially empty) project list.

The daemon runs until explicit stop: mnemos daemon stop. Want auto-start on login? See step 6.
quickstart.step4_body:
On the home page click "+ New project" (or right sidebar "All projects" → "+ New project").

The wizard opens. Fill in:
• Display name — any human-readable name (e.g., "Site builder").
• Slug — auto-derived (site-builder), used in URLs and paths.
• Vault path — folder where markdown pages will live. Click "📁 Browse" — picker opens with a "Computer" button to navigate drives.
• Project folders (CWD) — add through "+ Add folder" the place where you run Claude Code (e.g., D:\code\my-project). The "Include subfolders" checkbox adds a glob pattern (works recursively).

With an existing Obsidian vault: you can point to a folder where you already have markdowns — mnemos will just add new pages alongside the existing ones, won't touch yours. Recommended: make a manual git commit or snapshot before the first ingest, just in case.

Click "Create project". You'll be redirected to the project page.
quickstart.step5_title: 5. Run your first Claude Code session
quickstart.step5_body:
Go to the terminal in the folder you added as CWD: cd D:\code\my-project

Launch Claude Code: claude

Work for a few minutes — ask Claude something, get a reply. Close the session (Ctrl+D or /exit).

Return to the dashboard. Within ~10–30 seconds (Claude Code needs to save the JSONL and the watchdog needs to see it) the Overview project card will show a number under "Sessions with context".

If you launch Claude again in the same folder — at startup you'll get an injection of relevant pages from the vault.
quickstart.step6_body:
Want mnemos to come up by itself on every login?

mnemos tray install

This enables autostart + the icon right now. Per-platform details:

WINDOWS:
• Icon appears in the system tray (bottom-right corner of the screen, left of the clock). Don't see it? Click the "^" arrow in the tray, mnemos may be among the hidden items.
• Right-click on the icon → menu (Open dashboard / Restart daemon / Recent events / Quit).
• Left-click does nothing (Windows convention for system tray).
• Autostart implemented via .lnk in %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup\

MACOS:
• Icon appears in the menu bar (top-right of the screen, left of the clock / Control Center).
• Left-click on the icon — opens the menu immediately (macOS convention; menu bar items don't have right-click).
• Autostart implemented via launchd plist at ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.anthropic.claude-mnemos.tray.plist
• First launch may ask for permission (System Settings → Privacy & Security → allow mnemos-tray) — confirm.

Icon color on both OS:
• Green "M" — daemon alive, all good.
• Red — daemon crashed 3+ times in 5 minutes, supervisor stopped auto-restart attempts. Click "Restart daemon" in the menu manually, the counter resets.

Single-instance lock works on both — a second launch via .lnk/launchd will see ~/.claude-mnemos/tray.pid and exit cleanly, no duplicates.

Launch tray manually (without autostart): mnemos tray start.
Undo: mnemos tray uninstall — removes both the tray process and the autostart entry.

concepts.heading: Concepts
concepts.intro: What the dashboard's terms mean. Read once — then it's all intuitive. Ordered from "what gets added" to "what breaks" in logical sequence.
concepts.glossary_intro: Short definitions of recurring terms. Full explanations follow in the cards below.
concepts.glossary_def_backoff: An exponential pause between retries (e.g., 30s → 2 min → 20 min). Prevents hammering a failing service.
concepts.glossary_def_compression_ratio: Metric: full-context tokens divided by tokens actually injected by SessionStart hook. Higher = better context savings (5–10× normal, 20–50× in well-organized vaults).
concepts.glossary_def_CWD: Current Working Directory — the folder where you launch claude. Mnemos reads it from the session's JSONL to route the session to the right project.
concepts.glossary_def_frontmatter: A YAML metadata block at the top of a markdown page (between `---` lines). Holds type, status, confidence, tags, dates.
concepts.glossary_def_glob: Pattern syntax for matching paths: `*` matches any chars within a single folder, `**` matches across any number of folders.
concepts.glossary_def_HITL: Human In The Loop — nothing changes in the vault without your explicit approval (applies to ontology suggestions and risky operations).
concepts.glossary_def_ingest: The operation that turns a closed Claude Code session into atomic vault pages via an LLM. Runs after the session ends.
concepts.glossary_def_inject: The operation that adds the most relevant vault pages into a new Claude session's system prompt at startup. Runs before the session starts.
concepts.glossary_def_JSONL: JSON Lines — a text format where each line is one independent JSON object. Claude Code stores each session as a JSONL file (one event per line).
concepts.glossary_def_Levenshtein_distance: An algorithm measuring string similarity by counting single-character edits needed to turn one string into another. Used by lint autofix to suggest broken-wikilink replacements.
concepts.glossary_def_orphan_page: A page that nothing else in the vault links to. May be forgotten content; surfaced by the lint check.
concepts.glossary_def_pre_op_snapshot: An automatic full-vault snapshot taken before any risky operation (merge, delete, autofix). Lets you undo via Activity log.
concepts.glossary_def_provenance: The source attribution showing which session/source a page was extracted from. "Provenance dangling" = the source no longer exists in the vault.
concepts.glossary_def_retention_policy: An auto-delete rule based on age. E.g., snapshots default to 180 days; the trash defaults to 30 days. Configurable in settings.
concepts.glossary_def_wikilinks: Obsidian-style page links written as `[[Page Name]]`. Mnemos uses them to build the page graph for inject scoring.
concepts.projects_body:
A project is a named vault plus a set of CWD patterns. One project = one folder of markdown files + auto-routing rules for sessions.

You can have multiple projects. For example: one for work code, another for home experiments, a third for a book you're writing. Each has its own vault, memory, locale.

• slug — technical identifier (a-z, digits, dash, underscore). Used in URLs and file paths. Fixed at creation — cannot be changed.
• display_name — human-readable name for UI. Any characters, change as often as you like.
• vault_root — absolute path to the folder holding markdown files (e.g., D:\code\my-project\.mnemos or /Users/name/Documents/vault).
concepts.sessions_body:
A session is one Claude Code conversation, from claude launch to /exit or Ctrl+D. Technically — a JSONL file (JSON Lines — a text format where each line is one independent JSON object, here one line = one chat event) at ~/.claude/projects/<hash>/<uuid>.jsonl (Claude Code saves every conversation there automatically).

When the watchdog sees a new JSONL in a CWD folder of one of your projects — it queues an ingest job.

Ingest reads the JSONL, extracts content via LLM, creates (or updates) markdown pages in the vault. One session = 1+ new pages of type entity/concept/source.

See the session list: left menu → 💬 Sessions (inside a project). Shows session_id, token count, date, how many pages it produced, re-ingest button.
concepts.pages_body:
A page is a markdown file in your vault with frontmatter (a YAML block at the top of the file):

• type — entity (real-world object: tool, library, person), concept (idea, term, pattern), or source (1:1 session/article as sentinel).
• status — agent_written (model wrote it), human_edited (you edited by hand), archived.
• confidence — number 0..1, how sure the model is it wrote the truth.
• tags, last_updated, aliases (alternative names), and other fields.

Body — plain markdown. Connections between pages — through [[wikilinks]] (Obsidian syntax).

Open a page: menu → 📚 Pages → click the desired one. See the content, frontmatter, backlinks (who links to it). Or open the vault in Obsidian itself — see the graph.

Edit — "Edit" button at the top of the page (see workflow "Edit a page manually"). Mnemos doesn't block you from manual edits — on the contrary, after your edit the page gets human_edited status and takes priority over automatic updates.
concepts.inject_body:
The second half of mnemos magic. Ingest gathers info after a session, inject USES it before the next one. Without inject, mnemos would be just a chat backup.

How it works step-by-step:
1. You do cd /path/to/project && claude in a terminal.
2. Claude Code calls SessionStart hook (~/.claude/hooks/session-start.py).
3. The hook reads the session's cwd, finds the matching project via CWD patterns.
4. Builds a graph of all wiki pages in the vault via [[wikilinks]] between them.
5. Scores each page: the more it's referenced in others, the fresher it is, the closer it is to other important ones — the higher the score.
6. Takes top-N (by token budget — typically 3-8 pages) and inserts them into the system prompt as "raw context".
7. Claude sees this from the very start of the session — not as separate messages, but as built-in "project knowledge".

You DON'T need to do anything: the hook installs automatically when you do mnemos project add. Works transparently, you don't notice it.

What this gives in practice:
• Claude knows the names of your custom classes and functions from past sessions.
• Doesn't ask "what's your stack?" twice — remembers.
• Orients in what you already tried that didn't work (from corresponding mistake/decision/lesson pages).

The compression_ratio metric (visible on the 📈 Metrics page) shows how well inject works: full_tokens / actual_tokens. The higher — the better you save context. 5-10× is the norm; 20-50× is for well-"crystallized" projects.

How to verify inject works: open Claude Code, run /context — see if there's a section with your pages. Or check the supervisor log — every hook trigger is recorded there.
concepts.cwd_body:
CWD = Current Working Directory. When you do cd D:\code\my-project && claude in a terminal — how does mnemos know which project to ingest this session into? And how does the hook find the right vault for inject?

Via CWD patterns. Each project's settings list glob patterns (the glob spec = the same syntax bash uses for * and **).

• * — any characters within a single folder. Example: D:\code\sites\* matches D:\code\sites\landing, but does NOT match D:\code\sites\landing\src.
• ** — any characters across any number of folders. Example: D:\code\sites\** matches EVERYTHING under sites recursively.
• No wildcards — exact path match.

What the watchdog matches: it sees the session's cwd in the JSONL, finds the first project in the list whose at least one pattern matches.

Example setup for project "sites-builder":
  D:\code\sites\**
→ any session that started in D:\code\sites\landing\src\, D:\code\sites\shop\components\, D:\code\sites\…\…\ goes there automatically.

If no pattern matches — the session ends up in "🔍 Lost" (see the concept nearby).

UI: build patterns via CWD Builder in settings — "+ Add folder" button, then a browser with deep-listing and an "Include subfolders" checkbox (which appends `**`).
concepts.watchdog_body:
Watchdog is the daemon component that watches ~/.claude/projects/ and automatically finds new session JSONL files.

How it works:
1. Every N seconds the watchdog scans ~/.claude/projects/.
2. If it sees a new or recently changed file — reads metadata (cwd, session_id, mtime).
3. Checks: does the session's cwd match any project?
4. If yes — queues an ingest job.
5. If no — adds it to Lost.

Modes (configurable in settings → watchdog):
• immediate — monitors all the time, reacts to file changes within 1-3 seconds. Best for active work. Default.
• lazy — scans once every 5 minutes. Saves CPU, but ingest is delayed.
• disabled — doesn't scan at all. Only manual ingest via UI / CLI.

Watchdog state visible on the 🩺 Health page: green "Watchdog running" / red "Watchdog down".

If watchdog is in disabled mode — new sessions won't appear in Sessions/Lost on their own. Check manually via mnemos session import or UI.
concepts.lost_body:
When you launch claude in a folder that does NOT match any project — the session can't auto-ingest. Where does it go?

The session JSONL still gets saved to ~/.claude/projects/<hash>/ — that's done by Claude Code itself. Mnemos sees the file but doesn't know which vault to write it into. Such sessions land in the "🔍 Lost" list (sidebar at the top).

What you see there:
• session_id, start date, file size, first words from the transcript (preview).
• "Import to…" button — pick a project from the list, mnemos starts the ingest into that vault.
• "Ignore" button — marks it as "not for any project", removes from the list (the physical JSONL is not touched — it's yours, not ours).

Typical scenarios:
• Experimented with Claude in a new folder without a project → later decided it's worth saving to an existing one → go to Lost, click "Import to…".
• Accidentally launched Claude in /tmp or in someone else's working folder → mark "Ignore" so it doesn't clutter.
• Old sessions, from before you created a mnemos project → can be imported in bulk.

Prevention: make sure project CWD patterns cover all the folders you usually work in. Better `D:\code\**` upfront than catching lost ones later.
concepts.suggestions_body:
The ontology engine periodically scans the vault and proposes (via LLM):
• merge two pages describing the same thing (merge_entities) — e.g., "React" and "React.js".
• rename a page when the name is outdated ("React" → "React 19").
• delete a duplicate or an obviously erroneous page.

Each proposal must be explicitly approved, rejected, or deferred. This is HITL — Human In The Loop, NOTHING changes in the vault without your "yes".

Find: sidebar → 💡 Suggestions. The card shows type, confidence (0..1), reason "why the model decided so", preview of the result. The "Approve or reject a suggestion" workflow below describes the process in detail.

If confidence is low (<0.5) — read the reason carefully. LLMs often confuse similarly-named but different concepts (React vs React Native vs Preact, Python vs PyTorch).
concepts.lint_body:
Lint is a structural check of the markdown vault. It looks for typical issues that arise when an LLM writes pages automatically without manual control.

What it checks (7 rules by default, configurable in settings → lint):
1. Broken wikilinks — [[Page Name]] pointing to a non-existent page.
2. Orphan pages — pages that nothing links to (might be forgotten).
3. Duplicate titles — two pages with the same name.
4. Missing frontmatter — page without a YAML block at the top.
5. Invalid frontmatter — fields that shouldn't be there, or wrong types.
6. Provenance dangling — provenance is the source attribution showing which session/source the page was extracted from. "Dangling" means the page references a source-session that no longer exists in the vault (deleted or never imported).
7. Semantic (optional, slow) — LLM check that titles adequately describe content.

Runs:
• On cron (default: every night at 03:00 UTC).
• Manually via UI: 🩺 Health → "Run lint".

Result — a report with categories. Each issue has a type, severity (warning/error), a quote from the file (where exactly), and optionally — an autofix candidate.

Autofix:
• Broken wikilinks → search for a similar name (via Levenshtein distance — an algorithm for finding strings similar in spelling). If a similar name with high confidence is found — proposes a replacement.
• Duplicate titles → proposes a merge via a regular suggestion (doesn't execute immediately).
• Missing frontmatter → adds a basic template.

"Auto-fix all safe" button — applies only confident fixes (confidence >= 0.9), risky ones are left for manual confirmation.
concepts.snapshots_body:
A snapshot is a full vault copy at a specific moment. Made in three cases:
• Pre-op — automatically before any risky operation (merge, delete, autofix). If something went wrong — roll back via Activity.
• Daily — once per day on cron (default 04:00 UTC).
• Manual — on "Create snapshot" button click.

Storage: <vault>/snapshots/. Old ones cleaned by retention policy (a retention policy is an auto-delete rule based on age — default 180 days, configurable in settings → snapshots → retention_days).

How to restore from a snapshot: 💾 Snapshots → click on a snapshot → see metadata (when, before what, size) → "Restore". Vault returns to that state. Current state before rollback also saves into a new manual-snapshot "before-restore" — so you can "undo the undo" if you change your mind.
concepts.trash_body:
Mnemos never deletes pages forever immediately. Any delete operation (via UI, via automatic ontology, via lint autofix) first MOVES the page to <vault>/.trash/.

Kept there for 30 days (by default, configurable in settings → lifecycle), then auto-cleaned.

In the UI you see it via sidebar → 🗑 Trash. Each entry's card shows:
• The path the page came from.
• Date and reason for deletion (manual / suggestion accepted / lint autofix).
• "Restore" button — returns to the original path.
• "Delete permanently" button (with typed-confirm — must enter the exact page name).

If you've deleted a lot and trash has grown — there's an "Empty trash" button (also with typed-confirm).

The "soft delete" concept means you can confidently click "Delete" without fear of losing data — you have 30 days to change your mind.
concepts.activity_body:
Every operation mnemos performs on the vault — session ingest, page merge, lint autofix, manual edit, snapshot, restore — gets recorded in the "Activity" log (sidebar → 📜 Activity).

How this differs from Sessions:
• Sessions — your conversations with Claude (input data).
• Activity — WHAT mnemos did in response (changes to the vault).

What each entry shows:
• Operation type (ingest, merge, delete, lint_autofix, manual_edit, …).
• Time, duration.
• List of affected pages (with direct links).
• Metadata: reason (for automatic ones), who triggered it (for manual ones).
• Linked pre-op snapshot — if something went wrong, you can return EVERYTHING that changed in this operation.
• "Undo" button — reverses the operation (restores pages from the snapshot).

Scenario: you approved a merge proposal for two pages, a week later realized it was a mistake. You go to Activity, find that operation, click "Undo" — both pages return.

Grouping: today / yesterday / this week / earlier. Filter by operation type — useful if you want to see only automatic suggestions without manual edits.
concepts.deadletter_body:
If an ingest job failed, it first retries up to 4 times with backoff (30s / 2min / 20min — each next attempt with a longer pause). If it still fails — it lands in the dead-letter queue.

Open "⚠ Failed jobs" in the sidebar — see the list with error type, traceback, payload (what exactly was being executed). Buttons:
• "Retry" — puts the job back in the queue as fresh. Attempts counter resets.
• "Dismiss" — removes from dead-letter forever (with typed-confirm).

Most common causes:
• RateLimitError — subscription quota exceeded. Wait for reset_at or switch to API.
• NetworkError — internet glitched at request time.
• JSONDecodeError in extraction — bug in parser or corrupted transcript.
• DiskFullError — vault disk ran out of space.
• MissingApiKeyError — provider set to api but ANTHROPIC_API_KEY isn't set.
concepts.tray_body:
Mnemos-tray is a background process that owns the daemon and shows an icon in the system overlay.

Where to find it:
• WINDOWS: bottom-right of the screen (system tray), left of the clock. If there's a "^" arrow — click it, the icon may be among hidden ones. Color: green "M" = daemon alive; red = "Crashed" (3+ falls in 5 min).
• MACOS: top-right of the screen (menu bar), left of the clock / Control Center. Icon "M" — same green/red color code.

How to interact:
• WINDOWS: right-click → menu. Left-click does nothing.
• MACOS: left-click → menu immediately. There's no right-click for menu bar items in the system.

Menu (same on both OS):
• Open dashboard — opens http://localhost:5757/ in a browser.
• Restart daemon — softly restarts the daemon (without restarting the tray process). Crash-restart counter resets.
• Recent events — submenu with the last 8 lines from supervisor.log. Useful so you don't have to dig into the file.
• Quit — fully stops the tray process and the daemon.

What the supervisor (an internal component of the tray process) does:
• Starts the daemon on first launch.
• Checks every 5s that the daemon is alive via /api/health.
• If the daemon crashed — restarts with backoff (5s → 15s → 45s).
• If it crashed 3+ times in 5 minutes — stops automatic attempts, the icon turns red. Restart can be triggered manually from the menu.

Single-instance lock via ~/.claude-mnemos/tray.pid:
• On both OS checks if a process with this PID is running.
• If yes — second launch exits cleanly, no duplicates.
• Stale PID (from a process that crashed without cleanup) is automatically overwritten.
concepts.settings_body:
Project settings page: open via ⚙ Settings in the sidebar (when you're inside a project) or via home → click a project card → Settings.

12 accordion sections:
• General — display_name (editable), slug (read-only), vault path (editable with warning), CWD patterns.
• Locale — uk/ru/en or "inherit global".
• Auto-ingest, Lint, Ontology, Watchdog, Snapshots, Lifecycle, Prompts, Telemetry — all settings from §12 of the spec.
• Ingest parameters — overrides for global defaults (model, language hint, max tokens).
• Danger zone — delete project (typed-confirm).

Each section has its own "Save" button. Changes don't merge across sections.

Global settings — separate "⚙ Global settings" page in the sidebar (bottom). UI locale, daemon port, default LLM parameters.

workflows.heading: Common workflows
workflows.intro: What you'll do in the dashboard daily (and what — once a week). Ordered from most frequent to rare.
workflows.ingest_body:
By default — you do nothing. Closed Claude Code → watchdog saw a new JSONL → job queued → within a minute the page(s) appear in the vault.

If you want to make sure exactly what's being ingested, or force-run for an old session:
1. Menu → 💬 Sessions (inside the project)
2. Find the session in the list (you see session_id, date, token count, number of pages produced).
3. Click "Ingest" next to it — the job will be queued as fresh.

If the session is in "🔍 Lost" (CWD doesn't match any project) — open /lost-sessions, click "Import to…" and pick the destination.

Job state — in menu 🌊 Queue. Shows queued/running/completed/failed with progress.
workflows.edit_page_body:
Mnemos doesn't block you from editing — quite the opposite, it encourages it. Manual edits are recorded as human_edited and prioritized over automatic updates (the model won't overwrite your text when ingesting the next session on the same topic).

Via UI:
1. Sidebar → 📚 Pages → click a page.
2. "Edit" button at the top.
3. Editor opens: text with frontmatter on the left, preview on the right.
4. Changes → "Save". What happens:
   • If content changed noticeably — page status auto-flips to human_edited.
   • Recorded in Activity as manual_edit with a pre-op snapshot.
   • last_updated refreshes.

Via Obsidian (recommended for bulk edits):
1. Open <vault>/ as an Obsidian vault (File → Open vault → same path).
2. Edit normally — Obsidian saves files to disk.
3. mnemos has a file watcher: it sees the change, flips the page to human_edited.

What NOT to do:
• Don't change file names by hand — [[Old Name]] links will break. Use rename via UI or Obsidian rename instead (which updates wikilinks).
• Don't move between folders without checking backlinks.

Changed your mind — Activity → find manual_edit → "Undo". Restores the previous content from the snapshot.
workflows.approve_suggestion_body:
The ontology engine throws proposals like "two pages describe the same thing, merge?" or "the title has a small typo, rename?". All wait for your decision.

How to handle:
1. Sidebar → 💡 Suggestions.
2. The suggestion card shows:
   • Type (merge_entities / rename_entity / delete_page).
   • Confidence (0..1).
   • Reason (why the model decided so).
   • Preview of the result — what the vault will look like after applying.
3. Buttons:
   • Approve — performs the operation (with a pre-op snapshot). Can be undone via Activity.
   • Reject — marks as "don't do", removes from active. The model won't propose again (for N days — configurable).
   • Defer — bring back later (in N days).

If it's delete_page — there's another step: typed-confirm with the page name. Protection from accidental clicks.

Scenario: you see "Merge React and React.js — confidence 0.92, both describe the same library". You look at the preview — agree. Click "Approve". A snapshot is created, the React.js content merges into React, redirects remain, the React.js page itself is deleted to /trash.

If confidence is low (<0.5) — read the reason carefully. LLMs often confuse similarly-named but different concepts (React vs React Native vs Preact, Python vs PyTorch).
workflows.retry_failed_body:
If an ingest fell more than max_attempts times — the job lands in Dead-letter. This is NOT the end — it can be restarted.

Steps:
1. Sidebar → ⚠ Failed jobs.
2. Find the desired job in the list (by date, session_id, or error type).
3. Click opens details: full traceback, payload, attempt count.
4. Buttons:
   • Retry — puts the job back in the queue as fresh. Attempts counter resets.
   • Dismiss (delete) — removes from dead-letter forever (with typed-confirm).

Before just retrying — think why it fell:
• RateLimitError — subscription paused. Restart makes sense after reset_at time. Earlier — will fail again.
• NetworkError — internet glitched. Can retry immediately.
• JSONDecodeError in extraction — parser bug or corrupted transcript. Retry won't help. Open an issue with what the traceback shows.
• DiskFullError — free up space, then retry.
• MissingApiKeyError — configure provider (cli vs api), then retry.

You can retry in bulk: checkboxes next to jobs + "Retry selected" — useful after fixing a root cause (e.g., adding an API key).
workflows.snapshot_body:
A snapshot is taken automatically before merge, delete, lint autofix, and any operation that writes to the vault in bulk. You DON'T need to do this manually for normal work.

When you do need it manually:
• You're about to edit dozens of pages by hand.
• You want to lock in "everything's fine right now" before an experiment.
• Before upgrading mnemos to a new version (just in case).

How: sidebar → 💾 Snapshots → "Create snapshot" → write a comment (optional — helps find it later).

Restore: on the same screen click a snapshot → "Restore" → typed-confirm. Current state before rollback also saves into a new manual-snapshot "before-restore".
workflows.restore_body:
Pages deleted by mnemos don't disappear forever. They go to /trash with 30-day retention.

How to restore one page:
1. Menu → 🗑 Trash (inside the project).
2. Click on the page → see its content and metadata (where it came from, when and why deleted).
3. Click "Restore". The page returns to the original path.

How to restore an entire operation (multiple pages):
1. Menu → 📜 Activity.
2. Find the operation (e.g., a lint_autofix that deleted 5 pages).
3. "Undo" button — reverses the entire operation from the snapshot. All 5 pages return at once.

If everything's flying — sidebar → 💾 Snapshots → find the last stable one → "Restore". This is broader — returns the entire vault, not one operation.
workflows.lint_body:
Lint looks for structural issues in the vault: broken wikilinks, orphan pages, duplicate titles, missing frontmatter, provenance dangling. More details — in the "Lint" concept above.

Run manually: menu → 🩺 Health → "Run lint". Within a minute or two you'll see the report.

How to interpret the report:
• Categories: Errors (need fixing) and Warnings (optional). Each has a count.
• Click a category → list of pages with the issue. Each shows a quote from the file.

Autofix:
• "Auto-fix all safe" button — applies only confident fixes (confidence >= 0.9). A pre-op snapshot is created.
• If you don't like the result after autofix — undo via 📜 Activity → find lint_autofix → "Undo".

Recommended frequency — once a week for an active project. Or just rely on cron which does it every night.
workflows.metrics_body:
Menu → 📈 Metrics (sidebar bottom).

What you'll see:
• Token usage charts (input/output) over a period.
• Context compression (compression_ratio = full / actual). The higher — the better adaptive context works.
• Ingest frequency, biggest sessions by tokens.
• Per-project breakdown (if you have multiple projects).

Useful if you're on Pro and want to know how much time is left until rate limit, or just curious.

Period toggle buttons: 7d / 30d / 90d / 1y. Data aggregates per-day.
workflows.rename_body:
Just changing the human-readable name (display_name) — no problem:
1. Project settings → General → Display name → enter new → Save.
2. Sidebar and all titles refresh (via TanStack Query invalidation).

The slug CANNOT be changed — it's fixed at creation (because it's part of URLs and file paths). If you really need to — delete the project (no data loss — vault stays) and create a new one with the old vault and a new slug.
workflows.delete_body:
Settings → bottom "Danger zone" → "Delete project".

A modal opens with typed-confirm: you must enter the exact project slug for the button to activate.

What happens:
• Project disappears from the registry (~/.claude-mnemos/project-map.json).
• Settings ~/.claude-mnemos/settings/<slug>.json are deleted.
• IMPORTANT: The vault folder on disk is NOT deleted — markdowns remain. If you want — add the same slug + vault again and you'll get the project back.

If there are running jobs — you'll get a 409 with a "Force delete (cancel jobs)" link: jobs cancel, project deletes.
workflows.backup_vault_body:
The vault is your long-term memory — losing it means losing months of project context. Internal mnemos snapshots live next to the vault on the same disk; if the disk dies, the snapshots die too. So you need a real off-site backup.

Three complementary levels (use whichever fit your workflow — combine for safety):

1. Git (recommended for any vault you'd treat "like code")
The vault is pure markdown — git handles it perfectly, gives full history and easy diff:
  cd <vault>
  git init
  git add .
  git commit -m "vault init"
  git remote add origin <private-github-repo-url>
  git push -u origin main

What to put in .gitignore (don't commit these):
  .staging/
  .jobs.db
  .jobs.db-shm
  .jobs.db-wal
  snapshots/    # optional — big binary tarballs; either skip git or use git-lfs
  .trash/       # optional — if you want a tidy repo without deleted-items history

What to KEEP in git: wiki/ (your pages), manifest.json (ingest state — re-ingesting after restore won't duplicate). Commit cadence: once a day for active vaults, once a week for stable ones. Bonus: each commit is a recoverable state, redundant with snapshots.

2. Manual mnemos snapshot (internal, same-disk)
  mnemos snapshots create --comment "before-big-merge"

Good as a checkpoint before risky ops. NOT a real backup — same disk = single point of failure. Use as a safety net for short-term "undo", not for disaster recovery.

3. rsync / robocopy (off-site, fastest restore)
Mirror the vault directory to another disk (external SSD, NAS, USB stick):
  macOS / Linux:
    rsync -av --delete --exclude='.staging/' --exclude='.jobs.db*' \
      <vault>/ /mnt/backup/<vault-name>/

  Windows (PowerShell):
    robocopy D:\my-vault E:\backup\my-vault /MIR /XD .staging /XF .jobs.db .jobs.db-shm .jobs.db-wal

Schedule via cron (mac/linux) or Task Scheduler (Windows) — daily or hourly depending on volume. Restore = copy back over the empty target path, then reopen in mnemos.

What to skip on any backup level:
• .staging/ — in-flight transaction working files. Useless without their daemon process.
• .jobs.db / .jobs.db-shm / .jobs.db-wal — runtime job queue. Not data, just state. Backup will recreate clean queue.

What MUST be backed up:
• wiki/ — your pages. The actual content.
• manifest.json — ingest state (so re-ingest doesn't duplicate sessions).
• .trash/ — only if you want to restore deleted pages from before the disaster (optional).
• snapshots/ — optional, big. Often skipped because the rest of the vault already gives recovery; restore from a snapshot is a luxury.

If your vault path lives inside an Obsidian vault that itself uses git/Sync — you may already be covered. Verify by checking what your existing backup actually includes.
workflows.migrate_vault_title: Move the vault to another disk
workflows.migrate_vault_body:
When you'd want this: SSD running out of space, switching laptops, moving from a slow drive to a fast one, or restoring after disaster from a backup to a new path.

Step-by-step (do them in order — skipping a step risks data corruption):

1. Stop the daemon first.
  mnemos daemon stop
Don't skip this. If watchdog or an in-flight ingest writes to the old path during the copy, you'll get partial state on both sides.

2. Copy the vault directory tree fully.
  macOS / Linux:
    cp -a /old/path/to/vault /new/path/to/vault
    (the -a preserves permissions, timestamps, symlinks)

  Windows (PowerShell, recommended for big vaults):
    robocopy "D:\old\vault" "E:\new\vault" /MIR /COPYALL
    (/MIR mirrors, /COPYALL preserves all metadata + ACLs)

  Avoid drag-and-drop in Explorer/Finder for vaults >1GB — it loses NTFS attributes silently and makes restore weird.

3. Update the project's vault path in mnemos.
Open the dashboard → click the project → ⚙ Settings → General section → Vault path field → 📁 Browse → pick the new path → Save.
The daemon won't be running yet — settings save through the registry, not through a live mount.

4. Start the daemon back up.
  mnemos daemon start
Open the dashboard. Overview should show the project as healthy (no "mount failed" badge). Pages tab should list your existing markdowns.

5. Sanity-check before deleting the old vault. Bare minimum:
• Open one page from Pages tab — content renders, frontmatter parses.
• Click any backlink — navigation works (means the wikilinks index built fresh on the new path).
• Run a small ingest: cd into a project CWD folder, launch claude, ask one short question, exit. Within ~30s the new session should appear in Sessions list and produce a page somewhere under the new vault path.
• Check 🩺 Health — watchdog "running", no alerts.

6. Only then — remove the old vault directory. Keep it for a few days if you have the space; mnemos won't read it once the path is updated.

If step 4 shows "mount failed":
• The new path doesn't exist or is unreadable. Verify: ls /new/path/to/vault (mac/linux) or Test-Path E:\new\vault (Windows).
• On macOS — terminal/Python might lack "Full Disk Access". Grant it via System Settings → Privacy & Security → Full Disk Access.
• Path has a typo — re-edit Settings → Vault path.

Alternative path (if Settings flow misbehaves): delete the project via Danger zone (vault folder is NOT deleted from disk), then re-create the project from Onboarding wizard pointing at the new vault path. You'll keep the markdowns; you'll lose project-level settings (locale, ingest overrides) which you'll need to reconfigure. Use this only if direct path-edit failed.

troubleshooting.heading: If things break
troubleshooting.intro: Most common problems and what to do. Before opening an issue — try what's described here.
troubleshooting.daemon_down_body:
Check status: mnemos daemon status. If stopped — start: mnemos daemon start.

If still not working:
• Check port 5757 isn't taken:
  Windows (PowerShell): Get-NetTCPConnection -LocalPort 5757
  macOS / Linux: lsof -i :5757
• Check logs at ~/.claude-mnemos/: supervisor.log (the tray supervisor's own log of daemon start/stop/restart events with exit codes) and daemon.log (the daemon's stdout+stderr — full Python traceback when it crashes). Read both: supervisor.log says "the daemon died", daemon.log says why.
• A previous version of the process may be hung. Find and kill ONLY it, not all pythons:
  Windows: Get-Process | Where-Object { $_.ProcessName -like '*python*' -and $_.Path -like '*claude_mnemos*' } | Stop-Process
  macOS / Linux: ps aux | grep claude_mnemos then kill <pid>
  ⚠ DO NOT do `taskkill /F /IM python.exe` without a filter — it'll kill all your Python processes (e.g., jupyter, other projects).

If you have a tray icon:
• Windows: right-click → Restart daemon, or Quit + reopen tray.
• macOS: left-click → Restart daemon.
troubleshooting.ingest_failing_body:
Diagnostic steps (in order):
1. Check the session's CWD. A session that started in D:\foo\bar — does your project have a CWD pattern that matches D:\foo\bar? If not — create the matching pattern in project settings → General → Project folders.
2. Check /lost-sessions — maybe the session is there because CWD matches nothing. Then click "Import to…".
3. Check /dead-letter — if there was an LLM error, traceback is there.
4. Check the queue: menu → 🌊 Queue. Maybe a job is in queued/running right now — wait.
5. Check the watchdog: 🩺 Health → Watchdog state. If "down" — restart the daemon. If mode is "disabled" in settings — flip to "immediate".

If dead-letter has "MissingApiKeyError" — it means mnemos is in API mode without a key. Verify that:
• Either env has ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (the old way).
• Or the provider is set to cli: mnemos settings set --global ingest_provider=cli — then it goes through the Claude Code subscription (recommended path).
troubleshooting.inject_not_working_title: Inject doesn't work (Claude doesn't remember old stuff)
troubleshooting.inject_not_working_body:
Symptom: you launch Claude in a project folder, ask about something from old sessions — Claude doesn't know. Verification steps:

1. Is the hook installed? Check ~/.claude/settings.json — should have a hooks section with SessionStart pointing to mnemos.
   If not — reinstall: mnemos hooks install.

2. Does the hook fire at all? Run Claude with debug: claude --debug. In the output you'll see [hook] SessionStart triggered or an error.

3. Did it find a project? The hook logs triggers to ~/.claude-mnemos/inject.log. Check the latest lines. If "no project matched cwd" — your CWD isn't covered by patterns. Add a pattern in settings.

4. Are there relevant pages in the vault? If the vault is nearly empty (1-2 pages) — there's nothing to inject. Run 2-3 sessions in the project to accumulate.

5. /context in the session will show a "Mnemos context" section if inject worked. If it's missing but the hook logged a trigger — perhaps token budget=0 in settings, or all pages were filtered as stale.

6. Hook crashed with an error? ~/.claude-mnemos/inject.log will have the traceback. Most often — graph build on a very large vault times out. Increase timeout in settings → prompts.
troubleshooting.mount_failed_body:
On startup the daemon tries to mount each project from project-map.json. If the vault folder doesn't exist or there are no permissions — you'll get a "mount failed" error on Overview.

How to fix:
1. Check the folder exists:
   Windows: Test-Path D:\path\to\vault
   macOS / Linux: ls -la /path/to/vault
2. If not — create (mkdir), or change the path in settings via UI (⚙ Settings → General → Vault path → 📁 Browse → pick another).
3. Restart the daemon: mnemos daemon stop; sleep 2; mnemos daemon start.

If the project is orphan (vault disappeared somewhere, e.g., external drive disconnected) — either delete the project via UI (vault on disk won't be deleted), or just navigate to a new path in Settings.

Permissions (Permission denied on macOS): grant your terminal/Python full disk access via System Settings → Privacy & Security → Full Disk Access.
troubleshooting.no_subscription_body:
Mnemos needs an LLM to transform JSONL into markdown pages. Two paths:

Option 1: Pro/Max subscription (recommended)
• Buy Pro ($20/mo) at claude.ai/upgrade.
• claude login — authenticate.
• mnemos uses claude -p subprocess via the subscription. No extra token costs.

Option 2: Anthropic API key
• Sign up at console.anthropic.com ($5 free credit there).
• Set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in env or via CLI: mnemos settings set --global ingest_provider=api.
• Each ingest will deduct from your API balance (~$0.01–0.05 per session depending on size).
troubleshooting.tray_issues_title: Tray icon issues (ghost icons / icon disappeared)
troubleshooting.tray_issues_body:
WINDOWS: many "M" icons in the tray.
This is the shell32 ghost bug — Windows caches old icons that are actually dead. The real tray process is one (single-instance lock prevents a second).
How to clear:
1. Easiest: hover over each one — Windows redraws and removes the dead.
2. If many — restart Explorer (only affects the shell, not processes):
   PowerShell: Stop-Process -Name explorer -Force; Start-Process explorer
3. Still stuck — clear the registry cache:
   Reg key HKCU:\Software\Classes\Local Settings\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\TrayNotify → delete IconStreams and PastIconsStream values → restart Explorer.
4. Last resort: Windows reboot — guaranteed to clear the tray.

MACOS: icon disappeared from the menu bar.
• Check if the process is running: ps aux | grep mnemos-tray. If yes — click on the clock in the menu bar (sometimes menu bar items collapse due to a narrow screen; System Settings → Display → less dense resolution).
• If the process is gone — launch again: mnemos tray start.
• Check autostart: launchctl list | grep claude-mnemos. There should be an entry with a PID. If PID = 0 — it didn't start, check ~/Library/Logs/com.anthropic.claude-mnemos.tray.log.
• If the menu bar is crowded — macOS Sonoma+ auto-hides items. Show hidden ones: System Settings → Control Center → Menu Bar Only items.

MACOS: "cannot be opened because the developer cannot be verified".
Gatekeeper is blocking. Allow: System Settings → Privacy & Security → at the bottom there'll be "mnemos-tray was blocked" → Allow Anyway. Or zsh: xattr -dr com.apple.quarantine /path/to/mnemos-tray.
troubleshooting.tray_closed_title: Closed the tray (clicked Quit), how to bring it back
troubleshooting.tray_closed_body:
Quit fully stops the tray process AND the daemon. The dashboard won't open after this.

To bring it back:
• mnemos tray start — launches tray + daemon at once. Icon appears again.
• Or mnemos daemon start — launches just the daemon without the tray (enough to use the UI, but no auto-restart on crashes).

If autostart is installed (mnemos tray install) — your next system login will bring up the tray automatically, no action needed.
troubleshooting.vault_spaces_title: Paths with spaces (Program Files, Users\My Name)
troubleshooting.vault_spaces_body:
If your vault path contains spaces (D:\My Projects\vault or /Users/John Doe/vault) — most things work, but some CLI commands need quotes.

Windows (PowerShell):
mnemos project add foo --vault "D:\My Projects\vault"
(double quotes around the path)

macOS / Linux (bash/zsh):
mnemos project add foo --vault "/Users/John Doe/vault"
(also double quotes; single quotes work too, but \$ and ~ won't expand).

Via UI no problem at all — the picker passes the path correctly.

Unicode in paths (e.g., Cyrillic D:\Обсидиан\vault) — also supported. If accidentally not working — try double quotes explicitly, and ensure the terminal is UTF-8 (Windows: chcp 65001).
troubleshooting.rate_limit_body:
Pro gives ~45 messages per 5 hours. If you ingest many sessions in a row — you'll hit the limit.

Mnemos handles this automatically:
• The daemon catches RateLimitError → pauses the queue until reset_at (usually ~5 hours).
• On the dashboard (Overview) an amber banner appears "Rate limited — resumes at HH:MM".
• Jobs stay in queue (don't fall to dead-letter), just wait.
• When the pause is lifted — they continue from where they stopped.

Want faster — either buy Max ($100 = 5×Pro limits, $200 = 20×), or switch to API via ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (pay-per-use there, almost no limit).
troubleshooting.daemon_crash_loop_title: Daemon keeps crashing (red tray icon, supervisor gave up)
troubleshooting.daemon_crash_loop_body:
Symptom: tray icon turns red, dashboard becomes unreachable on every retry, supervisor.log shows the daemon restarted 3+ times within 5 minutes and then automatic restart attempts stopped.

Diagnostic order — read logs first, click "Restart daemon" last:

1. Open ~/.claude-mnemos/supervisor.log. Find the most recent block of restart_attempt entries — each one shows exit_code and a tail of stderr. Common patterns:
• `Address already in use: 5757` — port conflict (another process bound 5757). Find the offender:
  Windows: Get-NetTCPConnection -LocalPort 5757
  macOS / Linux: lsof -i :5757
  Kill it, or change the daemon port in Global settings → daemon_port and restart.
• `MissingApiKeyError` — provider mode is api but no key set. Either export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in env, or switch to subscription: mnemos settings set --global ingest_provider=cli.
• `database is locked` / errors mentioning .jobs.db — corrupted SQLite job queue. Stop the tray (mnemos tray uninstall), move ~/.claude-mnemos/.jobs.db aside (rename to .jobs.db.bak), restart. Daemon recreates an empty queue — you lose pending jobs but vault data is untouched.
• `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named claude_mnemos` — broken install (often happens when the tray uses a different Python than where mnemos is installed). Reinstall under the correct interpreter: pip install -e <path-to-claude-mnemos>.
• Permission errors on log/state files — happens after manual sudo runs that flipped ownership. Fix: chown -R $USER ~/.claude-mnemos.

2. Open ~/.claude-mnemos/daemon.log — the full Python traceback of the actual crash lives here. supervisor.log says "daemon died, exit 1"; daemon.log says why. Always read both.

3. Run the daemon in foreground to watch live: mnemos daemon foreground. This bypasses the supervisor entirely and prints everything to your terminal — quickest way to iterate on a fix.

4. Once you've fixed the root cause: tray menu → Restart daemon (this resets the crash counter), or fully recycle the tray: mnemos tray uninstall && mnemos tray install.

Don't just keep clicking Restart daemon without reading logs — the supervisor stops at 3 crashes specifically to force investigation. Repeated restarts of a broken daemon eat CPU and clutter logs.
troubleshooting.suggestion_bombardment_body:
Symptom: you reject "Merge React and React Native" → next ingest the same proposal appears again. Or the Suggestions list grows by 5+ similar low-confidence items per session and feels like noise.

Why this happens:
The ontology engine re-scans the vault after each ingest. Without your decision recorded as state it has no memory you already said "no" — the same heuristic fires and re-proposes. Or your confidence_min threshold is too low and the engine surfaces noisy candidates the LLM isn't sure about.

How to handle:

1. Always use "Reject" rather than just closing the tab or ignoring. Reject = marks the proposal with a deferred_until timestamp (default 30 days). Same pair won't be re-proposed until that period passes. Mere ignoring leaves nothing in state — guaranteed re-suggestion.

2. Tune confidence thresholds (Project settings → Ontology):
• confidence_min (default 0.5) — proposals scored below this are dropped silently, never shown. Raise to 0.7 if your vault has many similarly-named pages and you're tired of LLM confusion (React / React Native / Preact).
• confidence_auto_apply (default 0.95) — proposals above this auto-apply without HITL prompt. Lower to 0.9 only if you trust the engine on your domain (e.g., obvious case-difference duplicates).

3. If the same proposal type fires for many page pairs at once — usually it's a vault structure issue, not an engine issue. E.g., 20 "merge" proposals after a botched ingest creating duplicates: revert that ingest via 📜 Activity → Undo, then re-ingest after fixing the prompt or extraction settings.

4. Last resort: switch to manual mode (Settings → Ontology → auto_mode = manual). The engine stops periodic scanning; suggestions appear only when you run mnemos ontology scan from CLI. Useful while stabilizing a fresh vault — you opt back to auto once it's clean.

5. Don't use "Approve" as a way to dismiss noise — Approve performs the operation. To kill noise, always Reject (or Defer if you want to revisit later).

about.heading: About
about.body:
claude-mnemos — open-source project for Claude Code's long-term memory.

Report bugs at: https://github.com/<your-github-username>/claude-mnemos/issues (replace owner with your fork — project is currently private).
Documentation: docs/ in the repo.
License: MIT.
Requirements: Python 3.12+, Claude Code installed, OS Windows 10+ / macOS 12+ / Linux.

Want to contribute: PRs welcome. For big changes — open an issue first for discussion. docs/ has a spec describing architecture and immutable invariants.

picker.title: Choose folder
picker.truncated: Showing first 100 — refine filter to narrow

cwd_builder.empty: No folders added — sessions must be ingested manually

ignored_sessions.unignore_confirm_body: {{count}} session(s) will be moved back to the lost sessions list.

settings.title: Settings
settings.not_found_body: This project may have been deleted or renamed.

danger.title: Danger zone
danger.body: Permanent actions. The vault folder is not deleted; you can re-add the project to restore.
danger.modal_body: Removes the project from the registry. Vault folder at {{vault}} will NOT be deleted.
danger.force_modal_body: This will KILL {{running}} running job(s) and DROP {{queued}} queued job(s). Their work cannot be recovered. The vault folder remains on disk.

general.title: General
general.display_name_hint: Shown in dashboard. Leave empty to clear.
general.slug_hint: Used in URLs and file paths. Fixed at creation.
general.vault_hint: Absolute path to vault. Changing it only updates the registry — move existing data files manually before saving.
general.vault_warn: Path changed. The daemon does NOT move data files automatically — make sure the new directory contains your vault content (or is empty for a fresh start) before saving.

auto_ingest.title: Auto-ingest
auto_ingest.hint: Controls what happens to your Claude Code sessions automatically. Dump = copy .jsonl into the vault (free). Extract = LLM extracts knowledge into Markdown pages (paid, tokens).
auto_ingest.dump_on_session_end_hint: The SessionEnd hook copies the .jsonl into the vault (.ingest-jobs/). Cheap, no LLM. With this off, sessions are only reachable via the Lost-sessions page (manual import).
auto_ingest.dump_stale_after_24h_hint: A cron job at 03:00 picks up sessions that weren't updated for 24+ hours and were never dumped. Safety net for when the hook didn't fire (Claude crashed, etc.).
auto_ingest.extract_after_dump_hint: After each dump the LLM extracts knowledge into Markdown pages. Burns subscription/API tokens on every session — enable only for a fully-automatic pipeline.

lint.title: Lint
lint.hint: Scans vault pages for issues: broken wikilinks, duplicate titles, orphaned pages, etc. Runs manually from the Lint page by default.
lint.schedule_hint: Leave empty — lint never runs on its own. Example: "0 4 * * *" = every day at 04:00. Format: minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week.
lint.enabled_rules_hint: All ticked rules run on each lint pass. Uncheck only if a rule emits false positives in your vault.
lint.autofix_on_save_hint: After you edit a page by hand, findings flagged as "fixable" are applied automatically. Other rules stay informational.

snapshots.title: Snapshots
snapshots.hint: Archive of the whole vault into .snapshots/. Daily at 04:00 + before risky operations. Entries older than the retention window are pruned at 05:00.

system.heading: System
system.autostart_hint: Daemon starts automatically at login. Claude Code sessions are always captured.

row.hooks_ok: All Claude Code hooks are installed.

alert.claude_cli_not_installed: Claude Code CLI is not installed. Install it from docs.anthropic.com before using mnemos.
alert.hooks_not_installed: Claude Code hooks are not installed. Run `mnemos hooks install` so mnemos can capture sessions.
alert.hooks_partial: Some Claude Code hooks are missing: {{missing}}. Re-run `mnemos hooks install`.
alert.vault_not_writable: These vault roots are not writable: {{paths}}. Check permissions.

lint.title: Lint
lint.autofix_confirm_body: {{count}} fixable findings will be patched. A pre-op snapshot is created first; you can undo via Activity.

empty.title: Lint has not run yet
empty.body: Click "Run lint" to check this vault against the rule set. Findings are cached and reused until the next run.

clean.title: Vault is clean
clean.body: Last lint run produced zero findings. Re-run any time.
